title: The Wage
summary: Payment for labor by time or output — the form through which most people in a market economy get their living, and the hinge of the whole employment relationship. The wage looks like a simple price, but it encodes the central bargain of capitalism: what your time is worth, and who decides.
hook: Every paycheck tells a story. Not the story you tell yourself about what you earn, but a structural one: someone decided what an hour of your life was worth, and you agreed, because the alternative was worse. The wage is so ordinary that we stop noticing it. But it is not a natural feature of economic life. For most of human history, most people did not work for wages. They worked because they were enslaved, or bound to a lord, or farming land they could not leave. The wage — free labor, paid by time or output — is a relatively recent arrangement, and it rests on a question that has never been fully settled: is the deal fair?

beginner.theConcept:
Pick up a paycheck stub. There is your name, a number of hours, and a dollar amount. The math is simple. But behind it is a deal that shapes almost every working day of most people's lives.

A wage is money paid for work. An employer pays a worker a set amount for each hour, each day, or each unit produced. The worker shows up, does the job, and gets paid. That exchange is the foundation of how most people in market economies get their income.

Before wages became the norm, most people worked in other ways. Enslaved people worked under force, with no pay at all. Serfs in medieval Europe farmed land they were tied to, handing over a portion of what they grew to a lord. Craftsmen owned their own tools and sold what they made. The shift to wage work was slow, uneven, and often not a free choice. Peasants who lost access to common land had to find work somewhere. The wage was often what you took when the other options were gone.
beginner.problemItSolves:
The wage solves a coordination problem. An employer needs a building constructed, goods assembled, or customers served. A worker needs income. The wage connects them. You agree on a rate. Work happens. Payment follows.

Before reliable wages existed at scale, this was harder. Craft guilds regulated who could practice a trade and at what price. Barter required both sides to want what the other had, at the same time. Slavery provided labor but required brutal enforcement and gave workers no incentive to do more than the minimum.

The wage added something new: a price signal. If employers want workers badly enough, they compete by offering higher wages. If workers are plentiful, employers can offer less. In theory, wages move toward what the work is worth in a market. Whether the market reliably gets that right is the question everything else hangs on.
beginner.howItWorks:
Wages come in different forms. A time wage pays by the hour or the day. A piece rate pays per unit — you earn more by producing more. A salary is a fixed annual amount, usually split into regular paychecks, and typically covers professional or managerial work.

The difference matters. Piece rates reward high output but can push workers to skip breaks or cut corners. Time wages require employers to monitor whether workers are actually working. Salaries offer stability but can blur the line between work hours and personal time.

Most workers in wealthier countries also receive benefits on top of the base wage — health insurance, retirement contributions, paid leave. These are real compensation but rarely appear in the headline wage number, making fair comparisons across time and place harder than they look.
beginner.whatItExplains:
Two related concepts help explain what wages actually do: the nominal wage and the real wage. The nominal wage is the number on your paycheck. The real wage is what that number actually buys. If your paycheck rises 5 percent but prices rise 10 percent, your real wage fell. Real wages are what people actually care about, even when they argue about the nominal number.

In the United States and several other high-income economies after World War II, pay and productivity moved together more closely than they later would. As workers produced more, they earned more. That link started to break around the 1970s in the United States. Since then, productivity has continued to rise while median wages grew much more slowly. Workers produced more value but kept a smaller share of it. This gap between productivity and pay sits behind many current arguments about inequality and who the economy is actually working for.
beginner.whereItBreaksDown:
The basic model of wages assumes that workers and employers bargain as rough equals. Each side has something the other wants. They agree on a price. But the real labor market often does not look like that.

A single employer in a small town may be the only place to work for miles. Workers cannot easily move or switch jobs. In that situation, the employer sets wages rather than discovering them through competition. This condition is called monopsony, and it gives employers power to hold wages below what a competitive market would produce.

The gig economy shows a newer version of the problem. Companies like Uber and DoorDash classify their workers as independent contractors, not employees. That classification strips away overtime protection, minimum wage guarantees, and other rights. The worker faces the form of a free market — setting their own hours, working for multiple platforms — while carrying all the risk and little of the freedom that genuine self-employment offers. Courts in several countries have pushed back on this arrangement, but the legal lines remain contested.
beginner.usedAndMisusedC:
The oldest debate about wages is still alive: is the wage a fair exchange or a veiled form of exploitation?

Adam Smith saw the wage as a price set by supply and demand. Workers are scarce: wages rise. Workers are plentiful: wages fall. The market finds the right level.

Karl Marx disagreed. Workers sell their capacity to work — labor-power — not their actual output. The employer buys an hour and extracts more value from it than the wage covers. Marx called that difference surplus value, and called it the source of profit. On this view, the wage is not a fair exchange. It is how the output of labor gets divided, with the employer taking what the worker produced beyond their pay.

This is not settled. Whether the wage is a fair price or a tilt toward the employer depends on how much real choice workers have. The honest answer is: it varies, and it depends on power.

general.theConcept:
In the years around the Homestead Strike, Carnegie's steel workers faced long shifts, wage cuts, and a violent contest over whether their union would survive. Three years later, during the Homestead Strike, Pinkerton guards and state militia broke the union, and wages fell further. The episode shows the wage relationship under full industrial pressure: enormous productivity gains, limited worker leverage, and force when bargaining failed.

The wage has existed in some form across many societies, but it became the main way people got a living only with industrial capitalism. Before that, most people in farming economies worked under bondage, obligation, or subsistence. The move to wage work was not simply progress toward freedom. In many places it followed from the enclosure of common lands — when peasants lost access to shared fields, they had to sell their labor or starve. The freedom to sign a wage contract was real, but it arrived with a pressing need that made refusing difficult.

This origin matters. The wage is a contract, but rarely one between equals. Most workers need income more urgently than most employers need any specific worker. That gap in pressure is not a flaw in the system; it is part of how the system works. Laws, unions, and political action have all tried, with uneven success, to correct for it.
general.problemItSolves:
Adam Smith and David Ricardo both wrestled with what set wages. Smith observed that employers were better organized than workers in disputes, legally protected, and financially able to wait. Ricardo pushed this further with the idea of a subsistence wage — in competitive conditions, wages would tend toward what workers needed to survive and reproduce. It was a grim forecast for labor income.

Marx turned this observation into a theory. Workers sell labor-power — their capacity to work for a set period — not the output of that work. The employer directs the labor and takes what is produced. Profit is what remains after the wage. From this view, every wage is a partial payment; the rest goes to the employer by the logic of the arrangement.

Mainstream economics rejects the exploitation framing but admits the power problem. In a competitive market with many employers, wages should rise toward what an additional worker actually contributes. The trouble is that many labor markets are not competitive in this sense. When only a few employers operate in an area, workers cannot easily find a better offer. Lawrence Katz and Alan Krueger have argued that this kind of employer power — economists call it monopsony — is far more common than older models assumed. It helps explain why wages can stay low even when unemployment is low.
general.howItWorks:
For most of the twentieth century in the United States and Western Europe, wages and productivity moved together. As workers produced more per hour, their pay rose in rough proportion. Around 1973, this link broke. Productivity kept climbing. Median compensation — wages plus benefits — grew far more slowly. By the 2010s, productivity in the American economy had roughly doubled from its 1973 level while median pay had not kept pace.

Several things converged to open this gap. Union membership fell sharply from the 1950s peak: private-sector union density in the United States dropped from roughly 35 percent to under 7 percent by the 2020s. Unions had been the main way productivity gains fed through to wages. Their decline removed that link. At the same time, trade competition put pressure on manufacturing wages, and corporate governance shifted toward rewarding shareholders rather than workers. Each of these changes tilted the wage bargain toward employers.

Nominal wages matter less than real wages — what the paycheck actually buys. When pay rises slower than rents, healthcare, and education costs, workers feel poorer even if the headline number goes up. That gap is a major source of economic frustration across high-income countries today.
general.whatItExplains:
The wage explains much of what we observe in political economy. Why did the twentieth century see the development of minimum wage laws, overtime regulations, and collective bargaining rights? Because unregulated labor markets produced outcomes — poverty wages, dangerous conditions, child labor — that majorities eventually refused to accept. The regulatory architecture around wages is a record of what markets left to themselves produced, and a trace of the political battles fought to change it.

The entry of women into the formal wage labor market at scale across the twentieth century transformed the wage in ways still playing out. Women moved from unpaid household labor into paid employment, but the wages of female-dominated occupations have historically been lower than comparable male-dominated ones — a gap whose causes and persistence remain debated. The household economy entry is a closely related concept: the wage captures market labor, but enormous amounts of care work, child-rearing, and domestic production happen outside the wage system entirely.

Gig work and the platform economy add a new chapter. By classifying workers as independent contractors, platform companies shift risk — of illness, slow periods, equipment costs — to workers while retaining the direction and control that traditionally define employment. The legal and economic classification of this relationship is one of the sharpest wage disputes of the current period, with significant implications for how many workers in wealthy economies actually experience the employment bargain.
general.whereItBreaksDown:
The wage concept breaks down in two directions. From one side, much of the world's labor sits outside the formal wage system — subsistence farming, informal work, unpaid care, barter. The informal economy is enormous in lower-income countries and significant even in wealthy ones. Treating the wage as the normal form of labor income misses the majority of how labor is actually organized globally.

From the other side, the wage does not describe how the most highly paid people are compensated. Senior executives receive salaries, but the bulk of their pay comes as equity — stock options and grants whose value tracks company performance. This is a different thing from a wage: it aligns the executive with shareholders, not with the workforce, and it is taxed differently. CEO-to-worker pay ratios in large American companies reached into the hundreds by the 2020s. That extreme gap is partly a story about the wage's limits as a concept, and about who gets to decide how the returns from a business get divided.
general.usedAndMisusedC:
The fair-exchange-versus-exploitation question never goes away because it cannot be settled by evidence alone. Both positions fit the data. Markets do generate wage signals that reflect scarcity — tech workers in short supply earn more; unskilled workers in oversupply earn less. But the wage also reflects power: who can organize, who can wait, who can afford to leave.

The ideological use of the wage concept cuts in different directions. Defenders of low wages often invoke the market: wages are what they are because that is what the work is worth. Critics of low wages invoke a different frame: wages are what they are because workers lack the power to claim what their work produces. Neither is simply wrong. The wage is a price, and it is a power relation. Which lens reveals more depends partly on the specific labor market and partly on what question you are asking.

The minimum wage — covered separately in the Federal Minimum Wage entry — represents one response to wage-setting power: set a floor below which no employer can go. The wage entry and the minimum wage entry are connected but distinct. This entry is about the general structure of the wage relationship; the minimum wage entry is about a specific US policy tool. The two concepts are descendants of the same problem but operate at different levels of analysis.

advanced.theConcept:
The most fundamental question in wage theory is not what determines the wage level but what the wage actually is — what kind of thing it constitutes. Three answers have structured the debate.

For neoclassical economics, the wage is the price of a factor of production: labor markets clear when the wage equals the marginal product of labor, the additional output generated by the last worker hired. This is elegant and tractable, but it requires strong assumptions — competitive markets on both sides, homogeneous labor, no monopsony, no search frictions. Empirically, those conditions are often absent, and the model's predictions about wage determination routinely fail in ways that suggest the assumed competitive structure is not the dominant real-world form.

For Marx, the wage is not the price of labor but the price of labor-power — the capacity to work for a defined period. The distinction is deliberate and consequential. If workers sold their actual output, exchange would be transparent. By selling time and capacity, workers agree to a production process they do not control and whose fruits they do not own. Surplus value — the difference between what workers produce and what the wage covers — is not a market failure or an extraction: it is what the capitalist purchases when they hire a worker. This reframes exploitation not as a violation of market exchange but as its normal functioning.

The third position, developed within institutional economics and more recently within labor economics proper, holds that wages are bargained outcomes whose level reflects relative power rather than marginal productivity or exploitation in the Marxist sense. When one side can wait longer, organize more effectively, or exit more easily, they get more of the surplus. The insight from monopsony theory — that labor markets systematically favor buyers when workers face limited mobility or job options — maps onto this: wages are not discovering productivity but rather reflecting the distribution of bargaining power, which is itself shaped by law, institutions, and labor market structure.
advanced.problemItSolves:
The wage-productivity divergence since the 1970s is the central empirical puzzle in contemporary labor economics. Between roughly 1948 and 1973, productivity and median compensation in the United States moved closely together. After 1973, they diverged. By the 2010s, productivity had roughly doubled from its 1973 level while median real wages grew far less. The surplus generated by increased productive capacity went somewhere — primarily to capital income, and within labor income, disproportionately to the top of the distribution.

Several mechanisms have been proposed. The decline of union density is the most studied: unions redistributed bargaining power toward workers, and their decline reversed that redistribution. Trade exposure is a second mechanism: competition from lower-wage production shifted the domestic labor demand curve leftward for manufacturing workers, whose wages and employment fell. The rise of monopsony — increasing employer concentration in local labor markets, which reduced workers' outside options — is a third mechanism increasingly supported by empirical work using matched employer-employee data. These mechanisms are not mutually exclusive, and the econometric literature suggests they each contributed.

What remains genuinely contested is the relative weight of these mechanisms and, more fundamentally, whether the divergence represents a market failure (imperfect competition, union undersupply) or a market result (workers' actual marginal products did not rise as fast as average productivity, because the gains were concentrated in capital-intensive or high-skill sectors). The honest answer from the evidence is that all these forces operated, their effects varied across industries and regions, and disentangling them requires assumptions that reasonable scholars dispute.
advanced.howItWorks:
The formalization of piece-rate compensation theory in economics has revealed a persistent puzzle. Piece rates should, in theory, maximize effort and sort the most productive workers toward employers willing to pay for high output. But piece rates are used far less often than the theory predicts, even in settings where output is measurable. The standard explanations — that piece rates increase risk for workers, increase quality problems, and damage cooperative norms — are all empirically supported but none is fully dispositive.

The deeper issue that piece-rate analysis surfaces is the incomplete contracts problem in labor. The formal wage contract specifies payment, hours, and perhaps duties. It cannot specify every relevant dimension of worker behavior — effort intensity, cooperation, quality care, innovation. Employers try to address this through supervision, incentive pay, culture, and the implicit threat of dismissal. Workers respond strategically to all of these. The actual wage relationship is therefore not a transaction but an ongoing negotiated process in which the formal contract is a starting point, not a complete specification. This gap between the written wage and the actual employment relationship is where labor economics and organizational sociology converge, and it is where most of the interesting questions live.

The gig economy challenge to wage theory is significant precisely because it exploits this gap. By structuring the relationship as one between a platform and an independent contractor, companies attempt to retain the direction and control that justify treating work as employment while avoiding the legal obligations that employment triggers. Courts in the UK, parts of the EU, and some US states have held that the substance of the relationship — not its formal classification — determines the applicable law. The wage concept is being reconstructed in real time through this litigation.
advanced.whatItExplains:
The feminist critique of the wage has two registers that are often conflated. The first is empirical: the persistent gender wage gap, which across OECD economies has narrowed significantly since the 1970s but has not closed. The residual gap — the portion unexplained by occupation, hours, or human capital differences — ranges across studies from roughly 5 to 20 percent depending on methodology and country, and its causes are contested. Occupational segregation, discrimination, and differences in negotiation behavior have all been proposed and partially supported.

The second register is structural. Silvia Federici and the wages-for-housework movement of the 1970s argued that the wage system's apparent universality concealed a systematic exclusion: the reproductive labor that makes wage labor possible — childbearing, caregiving, cooking, emotional maintenance — was performed predominantly by women and received no wage. The wage economy ran on this unwaged foundation. This critique does not simply call for paying women more; it questions whether the wage relation can be extended equitably or whether it requires a non-market sector to absorb the costs it does not pay for. That question is more urgent in the twenty-first century, as demographic aging in high-income countries increases care demands while care wages remain near the bottom of the distribution.
advanced.whereItBreaksDown:
The wage concept has a double boundary problem. Below the formal economy, the entire apparatus of wage analysis — marginal productivity theory, minimum wage policy, union contracts, labor law — loses traction. International Labour Organization estimates put informal employment above 60 percent of global employment and above 80 percent in lower-income economies. Workers in this sector may receive something like a wage, but it is unenforceable, untaxed, and unmeasured. The labor economics literature has almost nothing reliable to say about how this majority of global workers is actually paid, because the data that the field depends on — payroll records, tax filings, employer surveys — do not exist for them.

At the upper end, the wage form has been hollowed out from a different direction. Once executive compensation shifted decisively toward equity in the 1990s, the gap between what 'wage' describes and what actually drives top-end compensation became very wide. The relevant variable for a CEO is not their salary but the movement of the company's stock price. This means their financial interest is structurally identical to a shareholder's and diverges from that of the workers they employ, whose wages are a cost to be minimized. The wage form persists at the top as a legal technicality — a nominal salary that anchors the equity grant — but it no longer captures the economic logic of how returns are distributed. What fills the gap at both ends — what concepts would actually describe how labor is compensated outside the formal wage system and at its top extremes — remains an open problem.
advanced.usedAndMisusedC:
The exploitation debate has not been resolved, but it has been clarified. The Marxist claim that workers are exploited is not simply a moral assertion; it is a structural claim about the relationship between what workers produce and what they receive. Whether this constitutes injustice depends on premises about property rights, the justice of initial distributions, and what alternatives exist — premises that cannot be settled by positive economics.

Philosopher G.A. Cohen's work in the 1970s and 1980s reconstructed the exploitation argument in analytical terms and showed that it was defensible against standard economic objections, provided you accepted certain premises about self-ownership and the initial distribution of productive assets. John Roemer subsequently showed that exploitation, in Cohen's formalized sense, can exist without any labor process at all — it is a property of the asset distribution, not of the labor relationship specifically. This move both strengthened the underlying critique and severed it from the specific wage relation that Marx had centered.

The practical implication is that whether the wage is fair in any given case is not a question resolvable by theory alone. It depends on the background distribution of assets, the degree of competition in the labor market, the availability of exit options, and the institutional framework. Wages set in a competitive market among workers with good outside options and in an economy with fair background distributions look very different from wages set by a dominant employer over workers with no exit in an economy shaped by centuries of dispossession. Treating both as instances of the same neutral market transaction is the ideological move the wage concept enables — and the thing that critics of that concept persistently resist.

foundation.theStory: The wage matters as the form through which most people in modern economies receive their income and as the institution that defines the employment relationship under capitalism. It is the point where economic structure and daily life intersect most directly. Understanding the wage means understanding how labor markets work, how power operates within them, and why the question of whether wages are fair has been so persistently contested across two centuries of economic argument.
foundation.theDebate: The central debates concern what determines the wage level (marginal productivity, bargaining power, or exploitation of the structural imbalance between workers and employers); why real wages diverged from productivity growth after the 1970s; whether the gig economy represents a genuine new form of work or a mechanism for evading wage protections; and whether the wage relation inherently involves exploitation or can constitute genuine fair exchange.
foundation.whyItMatters: The wage matters for understanding capitalism's structure, the history of labor movements, contemporary inequality, the feminist critique of unpaid care work, the political economy of trade unions and collective bargaining, and the legal status of new forms of work in the platform economy.
questions: If productivity and wages grew together for decades and then diverged, does this prove that wages were always driven by bargaining power rather than productivity — or does it suggest that something changed in the balance of power around 1973? What evidence would help you decide?

Gig workers do work that looks like employment (following platform rules, serving platform customers, wearing platform uniforms) but are classified as independent contractors. If the legal form and the economic substance point in opposite directions, which should determine workers' rights — and who should decide?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The essential classical texts are Adam Smith's Wealth of Nations (wage determination by bargaining), David Ricardo's Principles of Political Economy (subsistence wage theory), and Marx's Capital and Wages, Price and Profit (labor-power and surplus value). The neoclassical marginal productivity framework is standard in economics textbooks; the most rigorous modern critique is the monopsony literature, particularly Lawrence Katz and Alan Krueger's work on labor market power. The wage-productivity divergence is documented by the Economic Policy Institute and analyzed in Lawrence Mishel's work. Silvia Federici's Caliban and the Witch provides the most sustained feminist account of the wage's relationship to unwaged reproductive labor. G.A. Cohen's Karl Marx's Theory of History and John Roemer's A General Theory of Exploitation and Class provide the analytical reconstruction of the exploitation debate.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: Wage data is among the best-documented economic series available: tax records, payroll data, and labor surveys go back over a century in most wealthy countries. The main limits are: informal wages are poorly measured; benefits are inconsistently counted; and occupational composition changes over time make long-run comparisons difficult. The wage-productivity divergence is empirically robust but the attribution of causes is contested — multiple mechanisms operated and their relative weights require assumption-laden methods to disentangle. The gig economy evidence base is still thin because classification disputes have made systematic data collection difficult.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A defensible scholarly position on the wage must resist both the pure market-clearing account (wages simply reflect productivity; the market is always right) and the pure exploitation account (any wage under capitalism is necessarily unjust). The strongest available position holds that wages are bargained outcomes shaped by institutional context, and that the conditions for fair wage-setting — competitive labor markets, worker mobility, transparent information, legal protections for collective action — are demanding and unevenly met. Where those conditions are absent, the wage form does not guarantee a fair exchange. The wage-productivity divergence since the 1970s is most parsimoniously explained by shifts in institutional power — union decline, increased employer monopsony, changes in corporate governance — rather than by productivity differences. This position engages the exploitation critique seriously without accepting it wholesale: the structural argument that wages are shaped by power is compelling; the conclusion that any wage relation constitutes injustice depends on contested premises about property and distribution.
questions: Cohen showed that exploitation in the Marxist sense can be defined without reference to any specific labor process — it follows from the asset distribution alone. If exploitation is really about who owns the productive assets rather than about what happens in the wage relation, does the wage entry matter analytically, or is it only a legal form through which a prior distributional injustice expresses itself?

The wages-for-housework movement argued that paying women for domestic labor would both recognize its economic value and reveal the wage system's dependence on unwaged work. Critics responded that incorporating domestic labor into the wage system would commodify care and extend market logic into domains that need different principles. Both positions have something right. What is a strong argument that resolves the tension?

research.topic: The wage-productivity divergence in the United States after 1973
research.content: Economic research — particularly from the Economic Policy Institute, Lawrence Mishel, and others using Bureau of Labor Statistics data — has established that a substantial gap opened between productivity and median compensation in the United States around 1973. Before that point, median compensation and net productivity moved closely together for roughly two decades. After 1973, productivity continued to rise while median real hourly compensation grew far more slowly. By the 2010s, the cumulative divergence was substantial. This finding is consistent across methodologies, though the exact gap depends on choices about which price deflator to use and whether to include benefits in compensation. The divergence is smaller but still visible in most other high-income countries. The causes are debated (see emerging item), but the fact of divergence is not seriously contested.

research.topic: Monopsony power as a driver of wage suppression
research.content: A body of empirical work since the 2010s — associated with José Azar, Ioana Marinescu, Marshall Steinbaum, and others — has used employer concentration measures and matched employer-employee data to argue that labor market concentration gives employers significant wage-setting power beyond what competitive models predict. Research using online job posting data found that employer concentration in local labor markets is associated with lower posted wages, even controlling for other factors. This line of research challenges the standard competitive model and provides a mechanism for the wage-productivity divergence that does not require productivity mismeasurement. The empirical approach is contested: critics argue that concentration measures are imperfect proxies for actual monopsony power and that alternative explanations for the wage-concentration correlation are not ruled out. The field is active and the findings are being refined.

research.topic: The real purchasing power of wages for most workers through most of history
research.content: The full distribution of wages and real compensation for ordinary workers across most of history cannot be recovered. What survives is scattered — estate records, guild accounts, fragmentary census data, literary accounts — and systematically biased toward formal, urban, and recorded work. Robert Allen's reconstruction of historical real wages using estimated consumption baskets for European cities is the most rigorous attempt, but it applies to urban male workers and specific skilled trades. The wages of rural laborers, women, children, informal workers, and workers outside Europe remain poorly documented and largely inaccessible. What can be established: nominal wages for specific trades in specific cities from roughly the fifteenth century forward in some countries. What cannot be recovered: the real wage distribution across most of the population in most of history, the value of in-kind components of compensation, or the wages of workers outside formal recorded employment. Any aggregate claim about historical living standards requires inference from very partial data, and scholars disagree about what the gaps justify concluding.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Unequal Bargain
comparativeNarrative.content: From the labor movement's view, the wage has never been a clean deal between equals. Workers need income before they can afford to wait for a better offer. Employers can hold out longer, switch suppliers, and absorb delays. That gap in staying power means wages without organizing tend to land close to what workers will accept under pressure, not what their work is actually worth. Labor history is a history of trying to correct this — through unions, minimum wage campaigns, and strikes that force employers to bargain seriously. When those tools weaken, wages fall behind. The drop in union membership across Western economies since the 1980s, and the stagnation of wages in the middle and lower part of the distribution, is exactly what this logic predicts.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Price of Labor
comparativeNarrative.content: From the classical liberal view, the wage is a price discovered in a market. When employers compete for workers, wages rise. When workers are plentiful and jobs are scarce, wages fall. Left to work, this mechanism tends toward wages that reflect what workers actually contribute. Interference — minimum wages set too high, union contracts that protect insiders at the expense of others, hiring regulations — can break the mechanism and leave some workers worse off. The point is not that current wages are always right, but that the market is the most reliable way to set them. Where wages are too low, the answer is to raise worker skills, increase employer competition, and remove barriers to mobility — not to replace the price signal with a mandated number.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Wage and Its Shadow
comparativeNarrative.content: Feminist economists have argued that the wage system has always run on labor it does not pay for. The cooking, cleaning, child-rearing, and care work that allow people to go to paid jobs each day have mostly been done by women, without market compensation. The wage made men look like the earners while women's work stayed invisible. If care labor had to be paid at market rates, the cost would reshape how employers, states, and families organized work and income entirely. The feminist critique of the wage is not only about paying women more. It asks what the wage system hides — and whether any economy built around market pay can ever adequately value the work that makes everything else possible.

comparativeSummary: These three perspectives agree that the wage is the main way most people in market economies get their income. They disagree fundamentally about what the wage relationship actually is. The labor tradition sees it as a bargain structurally weighted toward the employer, whose abuses have to be corrected by organization and law. The market tradition sees it as a price signal that, when left to work, reflects real productive contributions and is distorted by intervention. The feminist tradition sees it as a concept that obscures as much as it reveals, built on a foundation of uncompensated work that the wage system by design does not count. The same hourly rate looks like fair payment, a suppressed share of output, or a narrow slice of what the economy actually depends on — depending on where you stand and what you are able to see.

rabbitHole.label: Capitalism
rabbitHole.reason: The wage is the form labor takes under capitalism. Understanding capitalism's basic structure — private ownership of production, the commodity form, profit as the goal — is prerequisite to understanding why the wage relation takes the shape it does.

rabbitHole.label: The Industrial Revolution
rabbitHole.reason: The mass spread of wage labor as the dominant form of employment happened during industrialization. The factory system drew workers off the land and into wage relationships at scale for the first time in history.

rabbitHole.label: The Factory
rabbitHole.reason: The factory is the institution where the wage relationship took its modern form. The factory brought workers together under a single roof, under a single employer's direction, paid by time — the spatial and organizational form that defined what the wage meant in practice.

rabbitHole.label: The Federal Minimum Wage
rabbitHole.reason: The minimum wage is the specific US policy that sets a legal floor on the wage. It is the most direct political response to the problem this entry identifies — that unregulated wage bargaining produces outcomes many people consider unacceptably low.

rabbitHole.label: Inequality
rabbitHole.reason: The divergence between wages at the top and bottom of the distribution is one of the primary drivers of income inequality in high-income countries. Understanding inequality requires understanding the wage-setting mechanisms that generate it.

rabbitHole.label: Social Class
rabbitHole.reason: Class position under capitalism is largely a function of the relationship to wage labor — who must sell their labor, who employs others, who owns productive assets. The wage is the economic expression of class position.

rabbitHole.label: Labor Unions
rabbitHole.reason: Unions are the primary institution workers have used to offset the power imbalance in wage bargaining. Union density and wage levels have moved closely together across time and across countries — the connection is direct.

rabbitHole.label: The Strike
rabbitHole.reason: The strike is the ultimate instrument for translating worker collective action into wage gains. It makes the wage's dependence on power visible: wages rise when workers are willing and able to withhold their labor.

reference.author: Marx, Karl
reference.title: Wages, Price and Profit
reference.annotation: The most accessible statement of Marx's wage theory — written as a public lecture and free of Capital's full technical apparatus. Establishes the distinction between labor and labor-power and makes the surplus value argument in compressed, readable form. The essential primary source for the exploitation account of wages.

reference.author: Smith, Adam
reference.title: An Inquiry into the Nature and Causes of the Wealth of Nations
reference.annotation: Book I, chapters 6–10 contain Smith's wage theory — including the observation that employers are better organized than workers in wage disputes, a point that undercuts the pure market-clearing account even in the founding classical text. Essential background for understanding how the debate was framed.

reference.author: Mishel, Lawrence; Bernstein, Jared; Shierholz, Heidi
reference.title: The State of Working America
reference.annotation: The standard empirical reference for US wage trends — documents the wage-productivity divergence, union density decline, and distributional shifts in worker compensation across multiple editions. The Economic Policy Institute's data series underlying this work is the primary source for the divergence figures cited in the entry.

reference.author: Federici, Silvia
reference.title: Caliban and the Witch: Women, the Body and Primitive Accumulation
reference.annotation: The most important feminist historical account of the wage — argues that the transition to capitalist wage labor was built on the simultaneous exclusion of women's reproductive labor from the wage system. Essential for the entry's feminist critique section and for understanding what the wage concept obscures.

reference.author: Azar, José; Marinescu, Ioana; Steinbaum, Marshall
reference.title: Labor Market Concentration
reference.annotation: The key empirical paper in the monopsony literature — uses online job posting data to show that local labor market concentration is associated with lower wages. Source for the entry's claim that monopsony power is more pervasive in modern labor markets than older models assumed.

reference.author: Cohen, G.A.
reference.title: Karl Marx's Theory of History: A Defence
reference.annotation: The most rigorous analytical reconstruction of the Marxist exploitation argument — clarifies what the claim actually requires and defends it against standard economic objections. Source for the educational layer's engagement with the exploitation debate and Cohen's formalization.

reference.author: David Weil
reference.title: The Fissured Workplace: Why Work Became So Bad for So Many and What Can Be Done to Improve It
reference.annotation: The account of how outsourcing, franchising, and subcontracting split the employment relationship and reshaped how wages are set — the basis for the entry's treatment of modern wage structures and gig/piece-rate work.

reference.author: Anna Stansbury and Lawrence H. Summers
reference.title: The Declining Worker Power Hypothesis: An Explanation for the Recent Evolution of the American Economy
reference.annotation: A Brookings Papers analysis arguing that falling worker bargaining power, more than pure market forces, explains the post-1980 divergence of pay from productivity; supports the entry's framing of the wage as a power relation.

commerce.author: Ehrenreich, Barbara
commerce.title: Nickel and Dimed: On (Not) Getting By in America
commerce.note: The best place to begin if you want to understand what wages mean in practice — Ehrenreich worked minimum-wage jobs and documented how little room the numbers leave. No theory, just the arithmetic of daily survival on low pay.

commerce.author: Graeber, David
commerce.title: Bullshit Jobs: A Theory
commerce.note: Read this if you are interested in what the wage relationship does to people beyond the paycheck — Graeber's argument that a large share of paid work feels meaningless to those doing it raises uncomfortable questions about what wages actually compensate.

commerce.author: Weil, David
commerce.title: The Fissured Workplace: Why Work Became So Bad for So Many and What Can Be Done to Improve It
commerce.note: For how corporate restructuring — subcontracting, franchising, gig classification — shifted wage costs and risk downward in the past forty years. The best account of the institutional mechanics behind wage stagnation.

commerce.author: Mishel, Lawrence; Bivens, Josh; Gould, Elise; Shierholz, Heidi
commerce.title: The State of Working America
commerce.note: For the data behind the wage-productivity divergence — the comprehensive empirical record of how US wages, benefits, and working conditions changed across the postwar decades.

commerce.author: Federici, Silvia
commerce.title: Caliban and the Witch: Women, the Body and Primitive Accumulation
commerce.note: For the feminist account of how the wage system was built on unpaid reproductive labor — demanding but the essential scholarly text for understanding what the wage concept excludes.

popularCulture.title: The Jungle — Upton Sinclair (1906)
popularCulture.description: Sinclair followed a Lithuanian immigrant family through Chicago's meatpacking industry. The wages are real and the arithmetic is brutal — every raise gets eaten by a new fee or a season of no work. The book changed food safety law, which is not what Sinclair wanted. He wanted readers to see how the wage trap worked. Most saw the bad meat instead.

popularCulture.title: Norma Rae (1979)
popularCulture.description: A textile worker in a North Carolina mill organizes her colleagues for better wages and conditions. The film shows that winning a union contract is not a matter of argument but of endurance and collective courage under sustained pressure. The famous scene where she holds a hand-lettered 'UNION' sign while the machines go quiet captures what the wage struggle actually looks like from the floor.

popularCulture.title: The living wage movement
popularCulture.description: Since the 1990s, advocates have pushed the idea that wages should cover actual costs of living rather than legal minimums. The phrase 'living wage' turns up in city ordinances, corporate pledges, and election campaigns. It implicitly concedes that market wages can be legal and still not be enough — a quiet acknowledgment of the entry's central tension.

popularCulture.title: Dirty Jobs (Discovery Channel, 2003–2012)
popularCulture.description: Mike Rowe spent seasons doing low-wage, physically demanding work: septic systems, roadkill removal, sewer inspection. The show was popular partly because it treated this labor with respect. It also quietly raised the question the wage concept forces: why does the least pleasant and most essential work so often pay the least?

popularCulture.title: The Fight for $15
popularCulture.description: The campaign for a $15 minimum wage began with fast food workers in 2012 and spread into a national political movement. It reframed the wage as a moral question — not a price to be discovered but a minimum dignity to be guaranteed. Several states and the District of Columbia reached $15 by the early 2020s. The federal floor did not move.
